Hedge Funds Lectures 7

amoroman  Course Outline • Hedge funds structure • Hedge funds vs. mutual funds • Hedge fund strategies • Portable alpha and pure play • Performance measurement for hedge funds • Exposure to omitted risk factors • Hedge funds and the use of options • Fee structure in hedge funds • High water marks • Funds of funds • Role of hedge funds in the economy FINS 3640  Hedge Funds: Useful links • HFRI indices: https://www.hedgefundresearch.com/family-indices/hfri • Credit Suisse HF: https://lab.credit-suisse.com/ • Warren Buffet critique to high-end money managers: • https://finance.yahoo.com/news/buffett-investments-fools-game-181822533.html • Warren Buffet Hedge Fund bet: • http://fortune.com/2017/12/30/warren-buffett-million-dollar-bet/ • Renaissance technologies website: https://www.renfund.com/ • Compensation in Hedge Funds: • https://www.cnbc.com/2017/05/17/the-top-earning-hedge-fund-managers-rakedin-11-billion-last-year.html

FINS 3640 3   Hedge Funds - Structure • Hedge funds are private investment vehicles open to a limited number of wealthy or professional investors. • Limited number of investors. Investors must be accredited or qualified purchasers (legal terms for “rich, sophisticated investors”). Minimum capital requirements are high. • See Appendix A for the definition of accredited or qualified purchasers under the exemption from 40Act.

• HFs usually take the form of a limited partnership or limited liability company or trust depending on the domicile • In the US, hedge funds are structured as limited partnerships • General partner (GP): responsible for day-to-day management security • Limited partner (LP): investors liable only for amount they invest

• HFs are structured so they are exempt from regulation under the Investment Company Act of 1940. • They only need to comply with § 3(c)(1) or § 3(c)(7) • See Appendix A for the details of Investment Company Act § 3(c)(1) & § 3(c)(7)

• In contrast, mutual funds are overseen by the SEC because they are issuing publicly traded securities (i.e., not limited to accredited or qualified purchasers)

• This means HFs have a lot of flexibility about the way they trade, the instruments they can purchase (derivatives), taking on short positions, etc… relative to mutual funds

FINS 3640 4 • HFs usually take the form of a limited partnership or limited liability company or trust depending on the domicile • Master/feeder structure to minimize taxes for different parties, particularly US taxes of the earnings of foreign investors • In the US, hedge funds are structured as limited partnerships • General partner (GP): responsible for day-to-day management • Limited partner (LP): investors liable only for amount they invest • HFs are structured so they are exempt from regulation under the • Investment Company Act of 1940: Comply with § 3(c)(1) or § 3(c)(7) • Securities Act 1933: firms issuing publicly traded securities overseen by the SEC

Hedge Fund Mutual Fund • Transparency: Limited Liability • Transparency: Regulations Partnerships with minimal require public disclosure of disclosure of strategy and strategy and portfolio portfolio composition composition • Investors: No more than 100 • Investors: Number is not limited “sophisticated” and wealthy investors

FINS 3640 5  Hedge Funds vs. Mutual Funds

Hedge Fund Mutual Fund • Investment Strategies: Very • Investment Strategies: flexible, funds can act Predictable, stable strategies, opportunistically and make a stated in prospectus wide range of investments • Limited use of shorting, • Often use shorting, leverage, leverage, options options

FINS 3640 6  Hedge Funds vs. Mutual Funds Hedge Fund Mutual Fund • Liquidity: Have lock-up • Liquidity: Investments periods, require advance can be moved more redemption notices easily into and out of a • Compensation structure: fund Management fee of 1- • Compensation structure: 2% of assets and an Fees are usually a fixed incentive fee of 20% of percentage of assets, profits typically 0.5% to 1.5%

FINS 3640 7  Hedge Fund Strategies • Directional/Market timing: range from specialists in certain sectors to global macro seeking to capture global market trends. This style is directional and is net long or short.

• Non-directional: aims to be market neutral and usually extracts value from a diversified set of “arbitrage” opportunities. • Exploit temporary misalignments in relative valuation across sectors • Buy one type of security and sell another similar security • Strives to be market neutral

• Both approaches can deliver low correlations with traditional asset classes.

• Discretionary: Relies on a person’s judgment to determine trades.

• Systematic: More rules-based and relies on quantitative models. The extreme example is quant black-box high-frequency trading, eg Renaissance.

FINS 3640 9  Strategy: Convergence and relative valuation • Convergence versus relative valuation: Involves the purchase of an undervalued security and the simultaneous short sale of an overvalued security. • Both seek to exploit temporary misalignments in prices. • Convergence expects the misalignment to occur within a pre-specified time frame (ex: futures price versus spot price).

• Example: Convergence Trade in Fixed income – Classic LTCM Trade • 30-year bonds, when issued (“on-the-run”), are usually more liquid than 29.5year bonds • Yield is lower (price higher) • Convergence trade: buy a 29.5 year bond and sell the 30-year bond.

14 FINS 3640 14  Market Neutral Long-Short & Pure Play: Portable Alpha 1. Invest wherever you can find alpha 2. Hedge the systematic risk of the investment to isolate its alpha 3. Establish exposure to desired market sectors by using passive products such as indexed mutual funds, ETFs, or index futures • Transfer alpha from the sector where you find it to the asset class in which you ultimately establish exposure

• Such strategies are not risk-free arbitrage strategies but rather statistical arbitrage (i.e., Pure play). FINS 3640 15  Portable Alpha Suppose have identified a new crypto for which you

• Exposure to omitted risk factors with positive risk premiums: • Liquidity • Tail events • Market timing implies straddle type returns.

• Survivorship bias: HFs with poor performance stop reporting • https://www.dolphins-world.com/dolphins-rescuing-humans/ • Backfill bias: HFs will wait for a good track record before starting to report.

• Changing factor loadings Market timing implies changes in B over time

• Hasanhodzic and Lo: Hedge fund returns have serial correlation, a sign of liquidity problems, which explains the upward bias in the Sharpe ratios

FINS 3640 22   Liquidity and Hedge Fund Performance • Sadka (2010, JFE): Unexpected declines in market liquidity are an important determinant of average hedge fund returns. Funds with higher liquidity-risk betas have higher returns (about 6% annually).

• Santa effect: Hedge funds report average returns in December that are substantially greater than their average returns in other months. This enhances their annual incentive fees… • The December spike in returns is stronger for lower-liquidity funds. • Suggests funds buy additional shares of stocks they already own to push up their prices. Especially at the close of the last day of the month.

• Selection bias • The best HFs do not report to databases, eg Renaissance Medallion, Caxton, DE Shaw, SAC, Citadel, etc

FINS 3640 25   Hedge Fund Performance and Changing edits Factor Loadings B • Hedge funds are designed to be opportunistic and may frequently change their risk profiles • If risk is not constant, alphas will be biased in the standard linear index model

• Conclusions • Perfect market timing results in a nonlinear characteristic line and hence greater sensitivity to the bull market • Funds that write options have greater sensitivity to the market when it is falling than when it is rising • Nonlinear characteristic lines suggest many hedge funds are implicit option writers

FINS 3640 29  Hedge Fund Performance and Changing Factor Loadings: Option Trades Sh EFFIE • Buying/selling options changes the distribution of returns, while static measures, like the SR, capture only certain components of the distribution O • Selling a covered call truncates the RHS of the distribution and results in negative skewness, which is undesirable. This is not captured by the SR. • Factor regressions can also be manipulated. Furthermore they can be amplified by leverage • Many common dynamic hedge fund strategies can be considered to be replicating option payoffs. Also • Monoline insurers selling protection on CDO products [no remaining AAA left except Berkshire Hathaway Insurance Corp, started in 2007] • LTCM, bailed out 1998 • AIG selling protection via CDS, bailed out 2008

30 FINS 3640 30  Performance: Non-Linear Factor Exposure • Jurek and Stafford (2011) show that the performance of hedge Source: Figure 2, Jurek and Stafford (2011)

funds can be matched, in aggregate, by a simple strategy of writing (naked) out-ofthe-money puts, and taking leverage. The implied fee for this strategy is 3.6%, very close to the average HF fee of 3.4%.

Occasional huge losses when the Mkt crash HFs are thus heavily exposed to Tail Risk 32 FINS 3640 32   Tail Events and Hedge Fund Performance • Nassim Taleb: • Many hedge funds rack up fame through strategies that make money most of the time, but expose investors to rare but extreme losses (“The Black Swan”, “Fooled by randomness”)

• Examples: • The Black swan in Australia • The October 1987 crash: 20% drop in one day! • Long term capital management: Convergence trades on sovereign fixed income… but in 1998, Russia defaulted on its debt and prices diverged instead of converging. In august, total return loss was almost 10 standard deviations of annual returns.

FINS 3640 33  Contracts: High Fees • Standard 2/20: expense ratio of 2%, performance fee of 20%. Renaissance Medallion is 5/44 • Performance fee is calculated net of a benchmark, e.g. LIBOR (cash benchmark for absolute returns) or the S&P500 • A high-water mark is often applied to the performance fee. Performance fees are levied only when the NAV exceeds the highest NAV previously achieved, eg NAV(0) = 100, NAV(1) = 120, NAV(2) = 110. No performance fee is payable at t=2 and none applies until NAV > 120. • Sometimes the incentive fee is paid only if performance is above a hurdle rate • Sometimes withdrawal fees are charged • Incentive fees are linked to higher performance: Ackermann et al. (1999) report a SR increase of 0.15 moving from a fund with no performance fee to a 20% performance fee with no increase in volatility

performer fee  Contracts: Limited Liquidity • Minimum investments, e.g. $5 or $10 million • Redemption is permitted typically only quarterly. Often HFs impose redemption notice periods, where an investor must wait 3 months, say, after having giving notice before the money is received. • Lockup provisions, often 2 years, where investors cannot withdraw money • Gates provisions: if there are “too many” redemption requests, the HF manager can limit redemptions. Often invoked when more than 20% of the fund is being redeemed, and redemptions are pro-rated. • Suspensions: if a fund is dying, redemptions can be suspended in their entirety due to gates or suspensions

• Contrast 40-Act funds, which must provide (at least) daily liquidity

FINS 3640 36 • Incentive fees are effectively call options on the portfolio with: X(strike) = (Portfolio value) × (1 + Benchmark return) so Citrfforreso M

• The manager gets the fee if the portfolio value rises sufficiently, but loses nothing if it falls

• incentive fee equal to 20% of investment profits. • Benchmark against risk-free

FINS 3640 HE portfolio Value 37

• High water mark • The fee structure can give incentives to shut down a poorly performing fund • If a fund experiences losses, it may not be able to charge an incentive unless it recovers to its previous higher value • With deep losses, this may be too difficult so the fund closes

FINS 3640 38

• Funds of funds (feeder funds) • Hedge funds that invest in one or more other funds, providing an opportunity to diversify across hedge funds • Supposed to provide due diligence in screening funds for investment worthiness

FINS 3640 39 • Funds of funds (cont’d.) • Pay an incentive fee to each underlying fund that outperforms its benchmark even if the aggregate performance is poor. The investor can lose money and still pay incentive fees! • Diversification can hurt the investor

• In theory, funds of funds spread risk across several different funds, but operate with considerable leverage (on top of the primary funds themselves)

• If the various hedge funds in which these funds of funds invest have similar investment styles, diversification may be illusory FINS 3640 40   Example: Incentive fees in funds of funds • A fund of funds has $3 million invested in three hedge funds • Hurdle rate for the incentive fee is a zero return • Each fund charges an incentive fee of 20% • The (before fees) return of the aggregate portfolio of the fund of funds is -5% • Still investors pay incentive fees of $.12 for every $3 invested 20 0.04 X20 Nay Navy Nate if FINS 3640 41  The economic role of hedge funds • In efficiently inefficient markets what is the role of hedge funds? • Make capital markets more efficient • Market allocates resources in the economy so this is vital for the economy. Makes capital cheaper for good firms (with good uses for capital) and more expensive to bad ones (incentivizing a shift in their resources) • Improves CEO decisions (more informative prices and more monitoring by active investors) • Provide liquidity to markets • Diversifying returns for investors

FINS 3640 42  (not so) Recent research Asness, Krail, and Liew (2001, JPM) Malkiel and Saha (2005, FAJ)

FINS 3640 43  Malkiel and Saha (2005): “Hedge funds risk and return” • Backfill bias: hedge funds can join the database and report past returns if they chose to do so.

FINS 3640 44  Malkiel and Saha (2005): “Hedge funds risk and return” • Survivorship bias: The past returns you see now and what you do not see.

Note: for mutual funds the difference is just 4.29pp. FINS 3640 45  Malkiel and Saha (2005): “Hedge funds risk and return” • Consistency in performance

Like mutual funds, about a coin-flip. Latter research finds evidence of persistence though (ex: Kosowski, Naik, and Teo (2007, JFE))

FINS 3640 46  Malkiel and Saha (2005) main conclusions • Hedge funds have bigger survivorship bias than mutual funds (about twice as much) plus a backfill bias that mutual funds do not have

• Hedge funds have higher attrition rates (75% die during the 8-year sample)

• No persistence in performance

• The risk of hedge funds is clearly non-normal

• A side note: In 2004 more than half of trades were made by hedge funds

• Still: correcting for the bias, hedge funds have low volatility and betas, so they provide interesting alphas for investors.

FINS 3640 47  Asness, Krail, and Liew (2001): “Do hedge funds hedge?” • How do we correct for risk when assets are illiquid (ex: stales quotes)? Returns will look smoother than they are…

• Scholes and Williams (1977, JFE) propose running: Rt +1 = a + b 0 Rm ,t +1 + b1 Rm ,t + b 2 Rm ,t -1 + ... + b k Rm ,t +1- k + e t +1

• Then our corrected beta estimate would be: k bˆ = c å bˆ i =0 i

• Asness, Krail, and Liew apply this to hedge fund returns. FINS 3640 48  Asness, Krail, and Liew (2001): “Do hedge funds hedge?”

1) Betas to lagged market returns are highly significant. 2) True risk of hedge funds is two to four times higher than the one estimated from simple regressions 3) Most alphas become negative after adjusting for risk. FINS 3640 49   Appendix A: Exemption from 40-Act  Definition • Hedge funds are private investment vehicles open to a limited number of wealthy or professional investors. They are characterized by • Limited number of investors. Investors must be accredited or qualified purchasers (legal terms for “rich, sophisticated investors”). Minimum capital requirements are high. • Usually have limited liquidity, ie limit access to invested capital • Often employ leverage and use derivatives. They invest in a wide range of strategies often not available in other investment vehicles. • Manager fees are very high and often have a performance component • Are [relatively] unregulated [Dodd-Frank 2010 will change this] • Are not transparent

• HFs usually take the form of a limited partnership or limited liability company or trust depending on the domicile • Master/feeder structure to minimize taxes for different parties, particularly US taxes of the earnings of foreign investors • In the US, hedge funds are structured as limited partnerships • General partner (GP): responsible for day-to-day management • Limited partner (LP): investors liable only for amount they invest • HFs are structured so they are exempt from regulation under the • Investment Company Act of 1940: Comply with § 3(c)(1) or § 3(c)(7) • Securities Act 1933: firms issuing publicly traded securities overseen by the SEC

§ 3(c)(1) Funds § 3(c)(7) Funds Private Offerings (no solicitation) Private Offerings (no solicitation)

Limited to 100 “accredited investors” Unlimited for “qualified purchasers”, but to avoid registration under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, limited to 499 qualified purchasers Accredited Investors: Qualified Purchasers:
